title: Quiche lorraine
subtitle: Shortcrust pastry filled with bacon, cream and eggs.
excerpt: A blind-baked shortcrust case filled with smoked lardons, Gruyère and a rich custard of eggs and cream, baked until just set. The definitive version of the dish. Serves six.
sourceNotes: Quiche lorraine. The dish originated in the Lorraine region of France. The name 'quiche' derives from the German 'Kuchen' (cake). Early quiche contained only egg, cream and smoked pork; cheese was a later addition. References appear throughout 19th-century French regional cookery.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze baked and cooled. Reheat from frozen at 180°C for 20 minutes covered, then 10 minutes uncovered.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Bake the day before and reheat at 160°C for 20 minutes. Quiche slices well when cold.
recipe.temperatureNote: Blind bake at 200°C, fill and bake at 180°C.

glossaryTerms.definition: Baking a pastry case empty, lined with paper and weights (baking beans), before adding the filling. This prevents the base from becoming soggy.

content.text: A good quiche lorraine has three things: a short, crisp pastry case, a custard that is barely set at the centre, and lardons that are properly browned before going in. Most disappointing quiches have two of these. Get all three and the result justifies the effort.

Blind-baking the pastry case before filling is not optional. If you skip it, the base will be raw and grey. The pastry needs 15 minutes with the baking beans and another 5 minutes without them to dry out fully.

Rub {{unsalted-butter}} of cold butter into {{plain-flour}} of flour and {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt until the mixture resembles breadcrumbs. Add {{eggs}} beaten egg and 1 to 2 tablespoons of cold water; bring together into a dough. Flatten into a disc, wrap and refrigerate for 30 minutes.

Heat the oven to 200°C. Roll out the pastry to 3 mm thick and line a 23 cm tart tin, pressing into the edges. Trim the excess and prick the base with a fork. Line with baking paper and fill with baking beans. Bake for 15 minutes. Remove the paper and beans; bake for another 5 to 8 minutes until the pastry is pale gold and dry. Reduce the oven to 180°C.

Fry {{streaky-bacon}} of bacon lardons in a dry pan for 5 minutes until golden and the fat has rendered. Drain on kitchen paper. Whisk {{eggs}} eggs with {{double-cream}} of double cream, {{nutmeg-ground}} of nutmeg, {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt and {{black-pepper}} of pepper. Scatter the lardons and {{gruyere}} of grated Gruyère over the pastry base. Pour the custard over the top, filling to within 5 mm of the rim. Bake for 25 to 30 minutes until the custard is just set with a slight wobble at the centre.

items.fix: Blind bake fully: 15 minutes with beans, then 5 to 8 minutes without. The base should be visibly dry before filling.

items.cause: Oven too hot, or baked too long.
items.fix: 180°C is the right temperature. Remove the quiche when the custard still wobbles slightly; it sets further as it cools.

items.symptom: Pastry shrinking down the sides of the tin
items.cause: Pastry not chilled before baking, or stretched when lining.
items.fix: Always chill the pastry and let it rest in the tin for 10 minutes in the fridge before baking.

content.text: Lorraine is the region of France that borders Germany, and the quiche reflects that border: a French name on an Alsatian baking tradition, filled with the smoked pork of the region. It became a British lunch staple in the 1970s and 80s, sold in varying states of quality everywhere from farmers markets to motorway services. Made at home from scratch it is a different thing entirely.
